content.full_text:
California
SUPERSEDED IN PART
Fair P”litical
BY 1998 AMENDMENTS TO
REGULATION 18530
Practices Commission
November 3,
1993
Robert E. Leidigh
Olson, Hagel,
Fong, .Leidigh,
Waters, and Fishburn
Capitol Bank Center
300 Capitol Mall,
Suite 350
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-93—348
Dear Mr. Leidigh:
This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on September 16,
1993,
and
October 6, l93,
which was requested on behalf of the Senate Rules
Committee.
Your request pertains to Section 89001 of. the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”),
the “mass mailing” provisions of
the Act.
1
Since your request does not name a specific public
official on whose behalf you make your request,
we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.
2
-
Moreover,
please note that although you indicate that due to
time constraints your clients need to act prior to receipt of our
written advice,
pursuant to Section 83114(b),
only the written
advice from the Commission is a complete defense in any
enforcement proceeding initiated by the Commission,
and evidence
of good faith conduct in any other civil or criminal proceeding.
QUESTIONS
1.
Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of a single written
response regarding pending legislation to members of an
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
-2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—348
-
Page 2
organization who are listed in a computer printout enclosed with
the organization’s letter of opposition?
2.
Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of all of the
following in a single mailing:
a)
An envelope which displays a Senator’s name and office
address on t:he outside;
b)
An unsigned memorandum, using the Senator’s office
letterhead, which contains a single mention of the Senator’s na-me;
and
c)
A “rolodex card” which has the Senator’s name together with
the Senator’s capitol and district office addresses and telephone
numbers.
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Section 89001 does not prohibit the mailing at public expense
of a single written response to the members of an organization who
are listed in a computer printout, which is enclosed with the
organization’s letter of opposition, where the letter was written
on behalf of all the organization’s members.
-
2.
Section 89001 does -not prohibit the mailing of the envelope,
the memorandum on the Senator’s letterhead,
and a “rolodex card,”
where an exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions under
Regulation 18901(b)
applies to each item included in the mailing.
FACTS
-
The facts are as follows:
1.
Three State Senators and one Member of the Assembly received
a letter from the President of -a nonprofit citizens organization
which protested the passage of pending legislation.
The letter
concluded as follows:
-
We are sending the names of 1200 members who
oppose this Bill,
and there will be more.
Surely,
you will agree that homeoiners represent the most
-
solid element of our society.
It is shocking to
think that such a bill, which would take away their
most important legal right, has even been
introduced.
-
All of us urge you to do everything in your
power to defeat the passage of AB269.-
You were
elected to defend us and we are counting on you.
Thank you.
Enclosed with the letter was a copy of a computer printout
with the names and addresses of the organization’s members who


Our File No.
1—93—348
Page
3
)
opposed the bill.
The letter was only signed by the
organization’s president, but the letter indicated all of the
members listed in the printout urged the legislators to defeat the
passage of the bill.
The letter was not induced or requested by
any of the legislators who received it or anyone acting on their
behest.
2.
There is an “800 number” available for calling a Senator’s
district office by members of the public.
A State Senator wishes
to send a memorandum to his or her constituents advising the
constituents of the new “800” service.
The memorandum would be
unsigned on the Senator’s office letterhead, which contains the
Senator’s name.
The memorandum contains no other mention of or
reference to the Senator.
An enclosure which constitutes a “rolodex card” would be
included in the mailing to the constituents which would enable
them to easily locate the “800 number” and other pertinent
information in the future.
The “rolodex card” would include a
single mention of the Senator’s name and would include the
Senator’s capitol and district office addresses and telephone
numbers.
The memorandum and “rolodex card” would be sent in an
envelope whih displays the Senator’s name and office address on
the outside.
)
Mass Mailing Prohibition
In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act
to provide:
“No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense.”
The Commission adopted Regulation 18901 to
clarify which mass mailings were permissible and which were
prohibited under the Act.
Regulation 18901(a) provides that a
mailing is prohibited only if all of the following apply:
(1)
Any item sent is delivered,
by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business,
or post office box.
For
purposes of this subdivision
(a) (1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record,
or button, or a
written document.
(2)
The item sent either:
(A)
Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the mailing,
or
(B)
Includes the name, office,
photograph,
or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the


Our File No.
1-93-348
Page 4
)
agency which produces or sends the mail
ing,
and is prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer;
(3) (A)
Any of the costs of distribution
is paid for with public moneys; or
(B)
Costs of design, production,
and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid
with public moneys,
and the design,
production, or printing is done with the
intent of sending the item other than as
permitted by this regulation.
(4)
More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent,
in a single calendar
month,
excluding any item sent in response to
an unsolicited request and any item described
in subdivision (b).
Regulation 18901(a).
The members of the State Senate are state e1ectd officials.
Thus, they are public officials subject to the provisions of
Section 89001.
(Section.82048.)
Unsolicited Requests
The first question you pose is whether a single written
response from three State Senators regarding legislation which -is
the subject of a written communication sent by members of an
organization to the Senators
(and one Member of the Assembly)
constitutes a mass mailing under -the Act.
Regulation 18901 applies only to a “mass mailing” as defined
in the Act.
“Mass mailing” means over 200 substantially similar
pieces of mail,
but does not include mail which is sent in response
to an unsolicited request or any items that fall within any other
exception set forth in Regulation 18901(b).
(Section 82041.5;
Regulation 18901 (a) (4).)
Materials sent in response to an
unsolicited request are not subject to the mass mailing
prohibitions.
(Geioque Advice Letter,
No. 1-91-084.)
Therefore,
such materials are not restricted.
Regulation 18901(c) (4)
states an unsolicited request is:
(A)
A written or oral communication
(including a petition)
which specifically requests
a response and which is not requested or induced by
the recipient elected officer or by any third
person acting at his or her behest.
However, an
unsolicited oral or written communication
(including a petition)
which contains no specific


Our File No.
1—93-348
Page 5
)
reci-uest for a response, will be deemed to
constitute an unsolicited request for a single
written response.
Regulation 18901(c) (4),
(Emphasis added.)
Under the facts you have provided, the organization’s letter
and the computer listing attached to the letter constitute a
written communication
3 under Regulation 18901(a) (4) which is not
requested or induced by the recipient elected officers.
Your
facts indicate that the letter from the organization which is
signed by the organization’s president is written on behalf of the
-1,200 members whose names were included as an enclosure to the
letter.
Furthermore, the letter specifically mentions that all
the members whose names are included in the listing urge the
defeat of the legislation.
Thus, the organization’s letter and the enclosed listing of
members who urge the defeat of the legislation constitute a
written communication from all the members of the organization
under Regulation 18901 (a) (4).
Therefore, the written
coimuunicatiol-i would constitute an unsolicited request for a single
written response.
Thus, the recipient Senators may send a single
written response regarding the legislation without being subject
to the mass mailing restrictions of the Act.
Our conclusion would
differ, however, if the individuals of the enclosed computer
printout are not,
in fact,
4 making the unsolicited or written
communication.
Letter and “Rolodex Card”
The second question you pose is whether a mass mailing sent
in an envelope on which appears a single mention of a State
Senator and with which enclosures include a memorandum which
includes the name of the State .Senator and a “rolodex card” which
also includes a single mention of the State Senator is prohibited
under Section 89001.
Where multiple items are included in the
same mailing, all enclosures must meet an exception in Regulation
18901.
Since we are able to reach this conclusion on the basis of the
language in the regulation which pertains to a “written
communication”, it is not necessary to determine whether the
computer printout is a “petition” under the provisions of
Regulation 18901(a) (4).
The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)


Our File No.
1—93-348
Page
6
Regulation 18901 states in pertinent part:
(b)
Notwithstanding subdivision (a),
mass
mailing of the following items is not prohibited by
Section 89001:
(1)
Any item in which the elected officer’s
name appears only in the letterhead or logotype of
the stationery,
forms (including “For Your
Information” or “Compliments of” cards) ,
and
envelopes of the agency sending the mailing,
or of
a committee of the agency,
or of the elected
officer, or in a roster listing containing the
names of all elected officers of the agency.
In
any such item, the names of all elected officers
must appear in the same type size, typeface, type
color,
and location.
Such item may not include the
elected officer’s photograph,
signature,
or any
other reference to the elected officer,
except as
specifically permitted in this subdivision
(b) (1)
or elsewhere in this regulation.
(11)
A business card which does not contain
the elected officer’s photograph or more than one
mention of the elected officer’s name.
Regulation 18901(b) (1)
and
(b) (11).
An agency may include the name of an elected official in the
standard letterhead or logotype of the agency,
where the name of
the elected official appears solely on the standard letterhead or
logotype of the stationery,
forms,
and envelopes of the official’s
agency, or a roster listing containing the name of all elected
officers, provided they appear in the same type size, typeface,
type color,
and location.
(Regulation 18901(b) (1); Martin Advice
Letter,
No.
A—9l-423; Murphy Advice Letter,
No.
A-89—088.)
The
exemption may be extended where the letterhead or logotype
exceptions apply independently.
(Goldberg Advice Letter,
No.
A—92—474.)
On the basis of your facts, it appears that the inclusion of
a single mention of the official’s name in a standard envelope and
in a standard letterhead each independently satisfy the
- requirements of Regulation 18901(b) (1).
However,
any other
reference is prohibited unless another exception applies.
Regulation 18901(b) (11) currently permits the inclusion of a
“business card” which does not contain the elected officer’s
photograph or more than one mention of the elected officer’s name.
A “business card” is a card normally distributed to identify a


Our File No.
1—93—348
Page 7
person for future reference,
and is normally printed solely with
the person’s name, business, telephone number,
and business
address.
A similar reasoning may be applied with regard a
“rolodex card.
You indicate that the card solely contains a Senator’s name,
his or her capitol and district office addresses,
and the
telephone numbers, including the “800 number.”
In addition,
you
indicate that the purpose for including the card in the enclosure
is to enable the Senators’ constituents to easily locate the “800
number” and other pertinent information in the future..
Therefore,
we conclude that a “rolodex card” may be treated as a “business
card” under this set of facts.
However, please note in order to be consistent with the scope
of Section 89001 and with our prior advice which has narrowly
construed exceptions to Regulation 18901,
incorporation of any
exempted item under this regulation must be determined on a case—
by—case basis.
Also, please also note that
a. mailing disseminated for the
purpose of seeking office and paid for with public funds is
prohibited uder Section 85300 of the Act.
In addition,
we do not
provide advice concerning laws other than the Political Reform
Act,
such as other laws which may restrict the use of public funds
in political campaigns.
(See, for example, Penal Code Section
424.)
I trust this letter addresses your concerns.
If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322-590l.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
D
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
}
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page..

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of a single written
response regarding pending legislation to members of an
;
-2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—348
-
organization who are listed in a computer printout enclosed with
the organization’s letter of opposition?
2.
Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of all of the
following in a single mailing:
a)
An envelope which displays a Senator’s name and office
address on t:he outside;
b)
An unsigned memorandum, using the Senator’s office
letterhead, which contains a single mention of the Senator’s na-me;
and
c)
A “rolodex card” which has the Senator’s name together with
the Senator’s capitol and district office addresses and telephone
numbers.

CONCLUSION: 1.
Section 89001 does not prohibit the mailing at public expense
of a single written response to the members of an organization who
are listed in a computer printout, which is enclosed with the
organization’s letter of opposition, where the letter was written
on behalf of all the organization’s members.
-
2.
Section 89001 does -not prohibit the mailing of the envelope,
the memorandum on the Senator’s letterhead,
and a “rolodex card,”
where an exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions under
Regulation 18901(b)
applies to each item included in the mailing.
embedding.first_500_words: California SUPERSEDED IN PART Fair P”litical BY 1998 AMENDMENTS TO REGULATION 18530 Practices Commission November 3, 1993 Robert E. Leidigh Olson, Hagel, Fong, .Leidigh, Waters, and Fishburn Capitol Bank Center 300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-93—348 Dear Mr. Leidigh: This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of telephone advice provided to you on September 16, 1993, and October 6, l93, which was requested on behalf of the Senate Rules Committee. Your request pertains to Section 89001 of. the Political Reform Act (the “Act”), the “mass mailing” provisions of the Act. 1 Since your request does not name a specific public official on whose behalf you make your request, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 - Moreover, please note that although you indicate that due to time constraints your clients need to act prior to receipt of our written advice, pursuant to Section 83114(b), only the written advice from the Commission is a complete defense in any enforcement proceeding initiated by the Commission, and evidence of good faith conduct in any other civil or criminal proceeding. QUESTIONS 1. Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of a single written response regarding pending legislation to members of an 1 Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; -2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1—93—348 - Page 2 organization who are listed in a computer printout enclosed with the organization’s letter of opposition? 2. Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of all of the following in a single mailing: a) An envelope which displays a Senator’s name and office address on t:he outside; b) An unsigned memorandum, using the Senator’s office letterhead, which contains a single mention of the Senator’s na-me; and c) A “rolodex card” which has the Senator’s name together with the Senator’s capitol and district office addresses and telephone numbers. CONCLUSIONS 1. Section 89001 does not prohibit the mailing at public expense of a single written response to the members of an organization who are listed in a computer printout, which is enclosed with the organization’s letter of opposition, where the letter was written on behalf of all the organization’s members. - 2. Section 89001 does -not prohibit the mailing of the envelope, the memorandum on the Senator’s letterhead, and a “rolodex card,” where an exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions under Regulation 18901(b) applies to each item included in the mailing.
